meta.title: Leadership Style Test
meta.description: A self-reflection test that explores how you tend to lead or influence a team — decision-making, delegation, motivating others, and handling conflict — across 10 dimensions of leadership.

levels.label: Exceptional Leader
levels.desc: You lead with real skill across decision-making, delegation, and conflict — people trust your judgment and follow your example, not just your instructions.

levels.label: Strong Leader
levels.desc: You have solid leadership instincts in most situations. A few specific scenarios still challenge you, but your team can generally rely on you.

levels.label: Capable Leader
levels.desc: You generally lead well, but pressure, favoritism, or avoidance of hard conversations can sometimes get in the way.

levels.label: Developing Leader
levels.desc: Your leadership is inconsistent — strong in some dimensions, reactive in others. Deliberate practice in a few key areas could help a lot.

levels.label: Emerging Leader
levels.desc: Leadership moments — decisions, delegation, conflict — often catch you off guard or get avoided. Small, consistent changes could meaningfully shift this.

levels.label: Needs Growth
levels.desc: Your answers suggest leadership instincts rarely guide your responses right now. This is a good starting point for focused self-work.

questions.text: A decision needs to be made quickly with incomplete information. How likely are you to make a timely call rather than delay for more data?
You're weighing a decision that affects your whole team. How likely are you to consider multiple perspectives before deciding?
A choice you made turns out to be wrong. How likely are you to reverse course quickly rather than defend it out of pride?
You could make a big decision alone without consulting anyone affected by it, just to move faster. How likely are you to do this?
Two good options are available, and you need to pick one. How likely are you to make a clear decision rather than get stuck in analysis paralysis?
A decision under pressure could go either way. How likely are you to stay calm and think it through rather than react impulsively?
Your team disagrees with a direction you're leaning toward. How likely are you to seriously weigh their input before deciding?
You're deciding between a safe option and a bolder one with more upside. How likely are you to take the calculated risk when it makes sense?
A decision you need to make involves trade-offs that will disappoint someone either way. How likely are you to make the call rather than avoid deciding?
You realize you don't have enough information to decide confidently. How likely are you to seek out that information rather than guess?
You're choosing between two vendors, and the cheaper one has a slightly worse reputation. How likely are you to weigh reputation heavily rather than just cost?
A decision needs input from a specialist outside your usual circle. How likely are you to seek them out rather than decide without that expertise?
You're deciding under time pressure and your gut says one thing, but the data suggests another. How likely are you to weigh both rather than dismiss either?
A past decision of yours is being second-guessed by someone senior. How likely are you to explain your reasoning calmly rather than get defensive?
You need to decide how to allocate a limited budget across competing needs. How likely are you to make trade-offs transparently rather than avoid the hard calls?
A decision you're facing has no clearly 'right' answer, just different risks. How likely are you to choose and commit rather than stall indefinitely?
You're given veto power over a decision but the team has already reached consensus. How likely are you to respect their input unless there's a strong reason not to?
You could make decisions based on who shouts the loudest in a meeting rather than who has the best argument. How likely are you to do this?
A decision could be improved by waiting a day to sleep on it, but there's mild pressure to decide now. How likely are you to take the extra time when it's available?
You're deciding on a policy that will set a precedent for future situations. How likely are you to think beyond the immediate case?
A decision involves a moral trade-off, not just a practical one. How likely are you to weigh the ethical dimension explicitly?
Your instinct says to reverse a prior decision, but that means admitting you were wrong publicly. How likely are you to do it anyway?
You need to make a call that a mentor or predecessor would have made differently. How likely are you to trust your own judgment appropriately?
A decision affects people who aren't in the room to advocate for themselves. How likely are you to consider their interests anyway?
You're asked to decide quickly on something you'd normally research thoroughly. How likely are you to flag the limitation rather than pretend full confidence?
A decision has strong emotional weight for you personally. How likely are you to separate that emotion from the actual merits before deciding?
You could delay an unpleasant but necessary decision indefinitely, hoping the problem resolves itself. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding between consistency with past decisions and adapting to new circumstances. How likely are you to choose adaptation when it's clearly warranted?
A decision requires balancing short-term results against long-term health of the team. How likely are you to protect the long-term view?
You're the tiebreaker in a split team vote. How likely are you to make a clear call rather than avoid the responsibility?
A decision you're about to make would be easier to defend later if you documented your reasoning now. How likely are you to do that?
You realize a decision framework you've relied on doesn't fit this particular situation. How likely are you to set it aside and think fresh?
A decision has a clear 'safe' choice that avoids blame and a better choice that carries more personal risk. How likely are you to choose what's actually better?
You're deciding how to respond to an unprecedented situation with no playbook. How likely are you to use first-principles thinking rather than freeze?
A decision could be made faster by skipping a step meant to catch errors. How likely are you to keep the safeguard anyway?
You could let personal loyalty to a friend override merit when making a decision that affects the team. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding whether to escalate an issue to leadership or handle it yourself. How likely are you to judge that correctly based on the stakes?
A decision requires you to admit uncertainty publicly. How likely are you to be transparent about what you don't know?
You have the authority to make a unilateral call, but consensus would strengthen buy-in. How likely are you to invest the time to build it when possible?
A decision you're making will disappoint a vocal minority but benefit the broader group. How likely are you to still make the call that serves the majority fairly?
You're deciding on a matter where your own past mistake is relevant context. How likely are you to factor in that lesson honestly?
A decision needs a tiebreaker between two equally qualified candidates. How likely are you to use fair, relevant criteria rather than convenience?
You're pressured to make a decision that benefits a powerful stakeholder unfairly. How likely are you to resist that pressure?
A decision's outcome is genuinely uncertain no matter how much you analyze it. How likely are you to accept that and decide rather than over-analyze?
You could make a decision and then quietly ignore feedback that suggests it was wrong. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding how much autonomy to give your team on an important initiative. How likely are you to lean toward trusting them with real ownership?
A decision needs buy-in from a difficult stakeholder. How likely are you to invest extra effort in bringing them along rather than steamroll them?
You're deciding whether a rule should have an exception in a specific case. How likely are you to weigh the specific circumstances fairly?
A decision you're making could set a bad precedent even though it solves today's problem. How likely are you to consider that long-term cost?
You need to decide between two paths where one is popular but the other is right. How likely are you to choose what's right?
A task on your plate could be handled well by someone on your team. How likely are you to delegate it rather than keep it yourself?
You delegate a task, and the result isn't exactly how you would have done it, but it's still good. How likely are you to accept it rather than redo it yourself?
A team member is capable of more responsibility than they currently have. How likely are you to give them a stretch assignment?
You're overwhelmed with tasks, some of which aren't the best use of your time. How likely are you to hand them off rather than power through alone?
You delegate a task and need to check in on progress. How likely are you to trust them with space rather than micromanage?
A new team member is eager to take on responsibility. How likely are you to give them a real opportunity rather than only small tasks?
You're delegating a high-stakes task. How likely are you to still provide the right support and clarity, not just hand it off blindly?
You could avoid delegating important tasks because you believe 'no one does it as well as you.' How likely are you to do this?
A team member asks for more ownership over their work. How likely are you to grant it if they've shown readiness?
You delegate a task that later needs adjustment. How likely are you to coach them through the fix rather than take it back and do it yourself?
You're on vacation, and a decision comes up that your team could handle without you. How likely are you to let them handle it rather than jump back in?
A capable team member hasn't been given real authority yet. How likely are you to extend it deliberately?
You delegate a task and the person asks clarifying questions. How likely are you to welcome those questions rather than see them as a lack of capability?
A task you're delegating touches on a sensitive relationship. How likely are you to brief the person thoroughly rather than leave them exposed?
You're building a succession plan for your own role. How likely are you to actively prepare someone to eventually take it over?
A team member wants to try a task in their own way, different from your usual method. How likely are you to let them, if the outcome matters more than the process?
You've delegated a project, but a stakeholder keeps coming to you directly. How likely are you to redirect them to the person now responsible?
You could take back a delegated task the moment it hits its first obstacle, instead of coaching through it. How likely are you to do this?
A junior team member asks to be included in a higher-level decision as a learning opportunity. How likely are you to include them if feasible?
You delegate authority over a budget to a team member. How likely are you to give them real latitude rather than requiring approval for every small expense?
A team member delivers a delegated task late but with good reason. How likely are you to respond with understanding rather than frustration?
You're delegating a task that requires skills the person hasn't fully developed yet. How likely are you to pair it with the right support?
A team member proactively takes initiative beyond what was delegated. How likely are you to encourage that rather than rein it in unnecessarily?
You delegate a decision, and the person makes a reasonable call you wouldn't have made. How likely are you to support their decision publicly?
A big project needs to be broken into delegated pieces. How likely are you to match each piece to the right person's strengths?
You're delegating to someone who's new and eager but unproven. How likely are you to give them a real, if smaller, chance to prove themselves?
You could delegate a task but withhold enough context that the person is set up to struggle. How likely are you to do this?
A delegated task needs a status update. How likely are you to ask for a summary rather than dig through the details yourself?
You realize you're the bottleneck because too much still routes through you. How likely are you to redistribute that load deliberately?
A team member exceeds expectations on a delegated task. How likely are you to entrust them with bigger responsibilities going forward?
You're delegating an unpopular task. How likely are you to be upfront about why it needs to be done rather than dump it without context?
A team member struggles with a delegated task despite real effort. How likely are you to step in to support rather than judge their capability?
You delegate a task to someone who then delegates part of it further. How likely are you to trust that as long as outcomes are met?
A high-stakes task is available, and a less experienced but promising team member wants it. How likely are you to give them a shot with the right safety net?
You're delegating recurring responsibilities as your team grows. How likely are you to formalize those into clear ongoing roles?
You could delegate all the boring tasks while keeping the interesting ones for yourself. How likely are you to do this?
A team member you delegated to made a reasonable mistake. How likely are you to treat it as a learning moment rather than a reason to stop delegating to them?
You're delegating cross-functional work that requires coordination with another team. How likely are you to set the person up with the right connections?
A team member asks you to just tell them what to do rather than figure it out themselves. How likely are you to coach them toward independent problem-solving instead?
You delegate a task with a tight deadline. How likely are you to check in appropriately without hovering?
A team member has grown enough to no longer need your day-to-day involvement in a task. How likely are you to step back accordingly?
You're delegating a decision that involves some risk. How likely are you to clarify the acceptable risk tolerance rather than leave it ambiguous?
A team member takes on a delegated task reluctantly. How likely are you to understand their hesitation before pushing forward?
You delegate a task, and it's completed using a creative approach you hadn't considered. How likely are you to recognize and value that creativity?
You could delegate responsibility for a task while still making all the actual decisions yourself. How likely are you to do this?
A team member's plate is full, but they're too eager to say no to more delegated work. How likely are you to check their capacity before adding more?
You're delegating a task where failure would have visible consequences. How likely are you to still delegate it if the person is ready, rather than play it safe yourself?
A team member delegated a task further without telling you. How likely are you to address the communication gap directly rather than take back all delegation?
You're building trust in a new team by delegating early. How likely are you to start with meaningful, not just trivial, tasks?
A team member's delegated work needs course correction. How likely are you to give specific, actionable feedback rather than vague criticism?
A team member seems to have lost enthusiasm for their work. How likely are you to check in and try to reignite their motivation?
Your team just missed a big goal. How likely are you to rally them toward the next one rather than dwell on the failure?
Someone on your team does excellent work quietly. How likely are you to make sure their contribution is recognized?
You need to motivate a team through a tedious but necessary phase of a project. How likely are you to find ways to keep morale up?
A team member's personal goals don't perfectly align with the team's current priorities. How likely are you to find ways to connect the two?
Your team is exhausted after a demanding period. How likely are you to acknowledge that and adjust expectations rather than just push harder?
Someone underperforming needs encouragement rather than criticism to turn things around. How likely are you to lead with encouragement first?
You could rely mainly on pressure or fear of consequences to get your team to perform. How likely are you to do this?
A team member achieves something meaningful. How likely are you to celebrate it in a way that feels genuine to them?
Your team faces a genuinely difficult challenge. How likely are you to frame it in a way that inspires effort rather than dread?
A team member is going through a rough patch outside of work. How likely are you to show flexibility and support during that time?
Your team hits a milestone that took months of grinding work. How likely are you to mark the moment meaningfully rather than move straight to the next task?
A quiet team member rarely speaks up but does solid work. How likely are you to make sure they feel valued, not just the vocal ones?
Your team is competing against a much stronger rival. How likely are you to frame the challenge as motivating rather than demoralizing?
A team member is burned out but reluctant to say so. How likely are you to notice and proactively address it?
You're leading a team through a period with few visible wins. How likely are you to help them find meaning in the process itself?
Someone on your team doubts their own abilities despite good performance. How likely are you to consistently reinforce their competence?
You could motivate through comparison, publicly pitting team members against each other. How likely are you to do this?
A team member's personal circumstances change, affecting their availability. How likely are you to adjust their role thoughtfully rather than penalize them?
Your team just onboarded someone new. How likely are you to invest extra energy in making them feel like they belong?
A long project is entering its most tedious phase. How likely are you to inject some variety or recognition to sustain energy?
Someone on your team took a risk that didn't pay off but showed good judgment. How likely are you to praise the judgment despite the outcome?
Your team's work is largely invisible to leadership. How likely are you to make sure they still feel their contribution matters?
A team member is motivated by different things than you are. How likely are you to tailor your recognition to what actually matters to them?
Your team faces layoffs or uncertainty elsewhere in the organization. How likely are you to provide honest reassurance rather than false positivity?
Someone on your team hits a personal best, even if it's still below the team average. How likely are you to celebrate their individual progress?
You could withhold praise because you worry it will make people complacent. How likely are you to do this?
A team member is going above and beyond without being asked. How likely are you to notice and acknowledge it before they burn out unnoticed?
Your team is nervous about a big upcoming challenge. How likely are you to project calm confidence that helps steady them?
Someone on your team made a public mistake and is embarrassed. How likely are you to publicly reaffirm your confidence in them?
A team member's motivation seems tied to fear of failure rather than genuine interest. How likely are you to help shift that toward healthier motivation?
Your team just finished a huge push. How likely are you to protect their recovery time rather than immediately load on the next big thing?
Someone on your team wants recognition in a specific way, like public praise or private thanks. How likely are you to learn and respect that preference?
A team member feels like their work goes unnoticed. How likely are you to make a point of noticing it going forward?
Your team is skeptical about a new initiative. How likely are you to address their doubts honestly rather than just cheerlead?
You could motivate your team mainly through guilt, reminding them how much you've sacrificed for them. How likely are you to do this?
A team member's confidence took a hit after a tough review. How likely are you to help them rebuild it with concrete, honest feedback?
Your team completes a project that didn't get external recognition it deserved. How likely are you to make sure they know you noticed?
Someone new to leadership on your team is nervous about their first big presentation. How likely are you to build them up beforehand?
Your team is stretched thin across too many priorities. How likely are you to help them focus rather than just push through everything?
A team member is motivated by growth opportunities more than recognition. How likely are you to offer that as a form of appreciation?
Your team faces a demotivating setback outside anyone's control. How likely are you to reframe it honestly rather than pretend it's fine?
Someone on your team is quietly carrying more than their share of work. How likely are you to notice and rebalance it?
Your team's energy dips midweek during a long project. How likely are you to notice and address the dip rather than ignore it?
You could only show appreciation for your top performers, ignoring solid contributions from everyone else. How likely are you to do this?
A team member is anxious about an upcoming change to their role. How likely are you to reassure them with specifics rather than vague comfort?
Your team just avoided a disaster through hard work. How likely are you to acknowledge the save, not just move on to the next fire?
Someone on your team feels overshadowed by a more vocal colleague. How likely are you to make deliberate space for their contributions to be seen?
Your team is facing a slow, unglamorous rebuilding phase. How likely are you to keep morale up by being honest about the timeline and progress?
A team member thanks you for your support during a hard time. How likely are you to acknowledge how much their effort mattered too?
Two team members are in conflict that's affecting the team's work. How likely are you to address it directly rather than hope it resolves itself?
You're mediating a dispute where both sides have valid points. How likely are you to find a fair resolution rather than favor one side?
A conflict arises from a decision you made. How likely are you to own your part in it rather than deflect?
Team members are avoiding a necessary difficult conversation. How likely are you to facilitate it rather than let tension build?
You disagree with your own boss's decision. How likely are you to raise your concerns professionally rather than comply silently or complain to others?
A conflict on your team involves someone you personally favor. How likely are you to remain objective anyway?
You need to deliver a difficult message to your team. How likely are you to be direct and honest rather than soften it into confusion?
You could avoid addressing team conflict altogether, hoping it resolves on its own. How likely are you to do this?
A conflict is really about unclear roles or expectations. How likely are you to clarify those rather than just address the symptom?
You're handling a conflict where emotions are running high. How likely are you to create space to cool down before resolving it?
A conflict between team members stems from a misunderstanding weeks old. How likely are you to trace it back and clear it up?
You're mediating a dispute where one side is more senior. How likely are you to still weigh the facts fairly rather than defer to seniority?
A conflict has damaged trust between two team members. How likely are you to help them rebuild it deliberately, not just paper over the issue?
You disagree publicly with a peer leader in front of others. How likely are you to keep the disagreement professional rather than let it turn personal?
A team member complains to you about another without going to them directly first. How likely are you to encourage direct communication first?
You're handling a conflict that involves a protected characteristic like age, gender, or background. How likely are you to handle it with extra care and fairness?
A conflict resolution you facilitated seems to be holding. How likely are you to follow up later to make sure it truly stuck?
You could pick favorites in a conflict based on who you personally get along with better. How likely are you to do this?
You're addressing a conflict where both people are partially right. How likely are you to validate both perspectives rather than declare a winner?
A conflict escalates because of a language or communication style barrier. How likely are you to help bridge that gap patiently?
You need to have a conflict conversation you've been dreading. How likely are you to have it anyway rather than let more time pass?
A conflict involves a client or external partner, not just internal team members. How likely are you to represent your team fairly while preserving the relationship?
You're handling a conflict where emotions from outside work are clearly spilling in. How likely are you to acknowledge that context with compassion?
A repeated pattern of conflict keeps emerging from the same root cause. How likely are you to address the root cause instead of each individual flare-up?
You're asked to weigh in on a conflict you have no personal stake in. How likely are you to still engage seriously rather than stay disengaged?
A conflict resolution requires an uncomfortable compromise from you personally. How likely are you to accept that if it's the fair outcome?
You could publicly side with whoever agrees with you more often, regardless of who's right in a given conflict. How likely are you to do this?
A team member escalates a conflict to you that they could have resolved themselves. How likely are you to coach them to handle similar situations independently next time?
You're resolving a conflict where one party has a documented pattern of the same behavior. How likely are you to consider that history fairly?
A conflict occurs during a high-pressure deadline. How likely are you to still address it properly rather than defer it 'until things calm down'?
You're handling a conflict remotely, without the benefit of in-person cues. How likely are you to be extra careful about tone and clarity?
A conflict involves a mistake you personally made that affected the team. How likely are you to name that clearly as part of resolving it?
You're asked to enforce a resolution that one party still resents. How likely are you to check in on how they're adjusting rather than assume it's settled?
A conflict reveals a genuine gap in your team's process, not just a personality clash. How likely are you to fix the process, not just the relationship?
You're managing a conflict where confidentiality matters to one party. How likely are you to protect that while still resolving the issue?
You could resolve a conflict by simply telling both parties to 'get over it' without addressing the substance. How likely are you to do this?
A conflict resolution requires you to apologize to someone you lead. How likely are you to do it sincerely rather than half-heartedly?
You're facilitating a conflict between departments with competing goals. How likely are you to find a solution that serves the larger organization?
A conflict has gone quiet, but you suspect it's not really resolved. How likely are you to check in rather than assume silence means peace?
You're handling a conflict where one person is clearly more skilled at arguing their case. How likely are you to make sure the less articulate person is still heard fairly?
A conflict arises over credit for shared work. How likely are you to investigate fairly rather than assume who's right?
You're resolving a conflict that involves a mistake in your own judgment from earlier. How likely are you to acknowledge that honestly as part of the fix?
A team member is consistently the source of interpersonal conflict. How likely are you to address the pattern directly with them?
You're mediating a conflict where cultural differences are contributing to the misunderstanding. How likely are you to account for that context respectfully?
You could avoid conflicts involving your closest allies on the team, letting their behavior slide. How likely are you to do this?
A conflict resolution needs buy-in from someone who wasn't part of the original discussion. How likely are you to loop them in properly rather than assume agreement?
You're handling a conflict where the stakes are genuinely high for the business. How likely are you to stay measured rather than let pressure make it worse?
A conflict keeps resurfacing in slightly different forms. How likely are you to name the underlying pattern to the people involved?
You're asked to resolve a conflict quickly to move on to other priorities. How likely are you to still give it the time it actually needs?
A conflict resolution you facilitate benefits you personally in some way. How likely are you to be transparent about that potential bias?
You need to explain a complex decision to your team. How likely are you to communicate the reasoning clearly rather than just the outcome?
A team member seems confused about expectations. How likely are you to clarify proactively rather than assume they'll figure it out?
You have difficult news to share with your team. How likely are you to communicate it transparently rather than sugarcoat it?
Someone asks a question you don't have the answer to. How likely are you to admit that honestly rather than bluff?
You're communicating a change that will be unpopular. How likely are you to explain the reasoning behind it, not just announce it?
A team member gives you feedback about your communication style. How likely are you to take it seriously and adjust?
You're leading a meeting that's going off track. How likely are you to steer it back constructively?
You could withhold important information from your team because sharing it feels inconvenient in the moment. How likely are you to do this?
You need to communicate across different levels of an organization. How likely are you to adjust your message for each audience?
A team member misunderstands an instruction you gave. How likely are you to take responsibility for clarifying rather than blame their listening?
You're announcing a decision that reverses something you said previously. How likely are you to acknowledge the change openly rather than hope no one notices?
A team member communicates best through writing rather than verbal discussion. How likely are you to accommodate that preference when it matters?
You need to deliver feedback that's likely to be received defensively. How likely are you to prepare your delivery thoughtfully rather than wing it?
A miscommunication caused real damage. How likely are you to address it head-on with everyone affected rather than let rumors fill the gap?
You're presenting a plan with real uncertainty in it. How likely are you to be upfront about the unknowns rather than project false certainty?
A junior team member's question reveals they missed something you explained before. How likely are you to patiently re-explain rather than show irritation?
You're communicating with a team that speaks a different first language. How likely are you to simplify without being condescending?
You could use jargon or complexity to sound more authoritative rather than to actually communicate clearly. How likely are you to do this?
A message you sent was misread as harsher than intended. How likely are you to follow up to clarify your tone?
You have to communicate a decision you personally disagree with. How likely are you to represent it fairly rather than undermine it subtly?
A stakeholder needs frequent updates during a long project. How likely are you to proactively provide them rather than wait to be asked?
You're communicating a success that was mostly due to one person's work. How likely are you to name them specifically rather than credit 'the team' vaguely?
A meeting could have been an email. How likely are you to recognize that and save everyone's time?
You need to explain a technical topic to a non-technical audience. How likely are you to adjust your language rather than assume shared knowledge?
A difficult truth needs to reach someone above you. How likely are you to deliver it clearly rather than water it down to protect yourself?
You're asked a question in a public setting that would be better answered privately. How likely are you to redirect it appropriately?
You could communicate vaguely on purpose to avoid being held to a specific commitment. How likely are you to do this?
A team member's presentation needs constructive feedback before a big meeting. How likely are you to give it in time to actually help, not after the fact?
You're translating a top-down directive for your team. How likely are you to add context that helps them understand the 'why'?
A conversation requires you to say something the other person doesn't want to hear. How likely are you to still say it clearly, with kindness?
You've noticed a pattern of your emails being misunderstood. How likely are you to adjust your writing style rather than blame the reader?
A cross-cultural team has different norms about directness. How likely are you to adapt your communication style per audience?
You're asked to summarize a long, complex situation for busy executives. How likely are you to distill it clearly rather than overload them with detail?
A rumor is spreading that isn't quite accurate. How likely are you to correct the record clearly and promptly?
You're giving feedback sandwiched between compliments. How likely are you to make sure the core message doesn't get lost in the softening?
You could tell people what they want to hear in the moment rather than what they need to know. How likely are you to do this?
A team member asks a blunt question you'd rather not answer directly. How likely are you to still answer honestly rather than dodge it?
You need to communicate a schedule change that inconveniences several people. How likely are you to explain the reasoning rather than just announce it?
A message needs to go out during a sensitive or emotional time. How likely are you to consider tone and timing carefully?
You're asked to explain a failure to people outside your team. How likely are you to be accurate without unnecessarily throwing anyone under the bus?
A team member's body language suggests confusion during your explanation. How likely are you to pause and check for understanding?
You're communicating expectations for a new project. How likely are you to put them in writing so there's no ambiguity later?
A conversation with a peer has gotten tense over email. How likely are you to switch to a call or in-person chat to de-escalate?
You need to give praise that's specific rather than generic. How likely are you to name exactly what someone did well?
You could avoid giving feedback altogether because confrontation feels uncomfortable, even when it's needed. How likely are you to do this?
A team update needs to balance honesty about challenges with maintaining morale. How likely are you to strike that balance rather than lean too far either way?
You're asked a question you suspect is testing your position rather than seeking information. How likely are you to answer honestly regardless?
A written policy is being misinterpreted by your team. How likely are you to clarify it in plain language rather than just point back to the document?
You need to deliver the same message to different audiences with different concerns. How likely are you to tailor the emphasis for each?
A team member finally opens up about a communication style that isn't working for them. How likely are you to genuinely adjust rather than just acknowledge and continue as before?
A project you led fails to meet its goals. How likely are you to publicly own the outcome rather than blame your team?
You promised your team something and circumstances make it hard to deliver. How likely are you to communicate that honestly rather than go quiet?
A mistake happened under your watch. How likely are you to take responsibility for the oversight, even if you didn't make the direct error?
You set a standard for your team but haven't been meeting it yourself. How likely are you to correct your own behavior first?
Your team looks to you after a public failure. How likely are you to acknowledge it honestly rather than spin it?
You're asked to report on a project's status that isn't going well. How likely are you to give an accurate account rather than an overly optimistic one?
A commitment you made to a stakeholder becomes inconvenient to keep. How likely are you to honor it anyway?
You could let a team member take the blame for a decision that was ultimately yours. How likely are you to do this?
You realize a policy you created is causing problems. How likely are you to revise it and admit the original approach was flawed?
Your team's success depends on a decision you're unsure about. How likely are you to stand behind it and take responsibility for the outcome either way?
You're asked to sign off on results you haven't fully verified. How likely are you to check first rather than approve on trust alone?
A team you lead underperforms against a target you set. How likely are you to examine your own role in that before looking elsewhere?
You made a promise to a client that your team now can't fully deliver. How likely are you to communicate that proactively rather than let them find out?
A metric you report on shows unfavorable results. How likely are you to report it accurately rather than choose a more flattering metric instead?
You're asked why a project is behind schedule. How likely are you to give a candid account of the real reasons, including your own missteps?
A decision you championed isn't working out. How likely are you to be the one to flag it rather than wait for someone else to notice?
You're accountable for a budget that went over. How likely are you to explain why rather than obscure the details?
You could quietly rewrite the narrative of what happened to make your role look better in hindsight. How likely are you to do this?
A team member did something wrong under your leadership. How likely are you to also examine what oversight you could have provided?
You're asked to take a public stance on a decision that didn't go well. How likely are you to be straightforward rather than evasive?
A project's success is genuinely due to your team, not you personally. How likely are you to make that clear rather than accept undue credit?
You're being held accountable for something partially outside your control. How likely are you to explain the context honestly rather than either over-blame yourself or deflect entirely?
A key deadline was missed. How likely are you to conduct an honest post-mortem rather than quietly move on?
You're asked to justify a controversial call you made. How likely are you to stand by your reasoning transparently, right or wrong?
A commitment you made publicly is proving harder to keep than expected. How likely are you to update stakeholders rather than go quiet?
You notice you've been avoiding a piece of accountability that's uncomfortable. How likely are you to address it once you notice the avoidance?
You could accept credit for a win while distancing yourself from any associated risk or downside. How likely are you to do this?
A team initiative you own hasn't delivered results yet. How likely are you to give an honest timeline update rather than vague reassurance?
You're asked to account for a resource that went missing or was mismanaged. How likely are you to investigate thoroughly rather than assume it's not your problem?
A decision under your authority caused unintended harm. How likely are you to take responsibility for fixing it, not just acknowledging it?
You promised transparency to your team, and now an uncomfortable truth needs sharing. How likely are you to follow through on that promise?
A goal you set for the team turned out to be poorly calibrated. How likely are you to own that miscalibration rather than blame effort?
You're asked to explain a gap between what you said and what actually happened. How likely are you to close that gap honestly?
A high-visibility failure happened on your team. How likely are you to be the public face of accountability rather than let a junior member absorb the scrutiny?
You committed to a standard of behavior for yourself as a leader. How likely are you to hold yourself to it even when it's inconvenient?
You could quietly lower your own standards while still holding your team to the original ones. How likely are you to do this?
A project you're accountable for depended on another team that fell through. How likely are you to still take ownership of the overall outcome?
You're asked to reflect on a leadership mistake in a public forum. How likely are you to be genuinely reflective rather than give a rehearsed non-answer?
A decision you made in good faith turned out to have a bad side effect you didn't foresee. How likely are you to acknowledge that oversight honestly?
You're accountable to multiple stakeholders with different expectations. How likely are you to be transparent with all of them rather than tell each what they want to hear?
A team member points out that a policy you set is causing problems. How likely are you to take that feedback seriously rather than defend it reflexively?
You said you'd handle a sensitive issue personally. How likely are you to actually follow through rather than quietly hand it off?
An audit or review of your area turns up issues. How likely are you to engage constructively rather than treat it as an attack?
You're asked what you'd do differently in hindsight. How likely are you to give a genuine answer rather than a diplomatic non-answer?
You could let ambiguity about who's responsible protect you from being blamed for an outcome. How likely are you to lean into that ambiguity?
A commitment to change your own behavior, based on past feedback, is being tested. How likely are you to actually follow through under pressure?
You're asked to account for how a decision aligns with the values you've publicly stated. How likely are you to hold that comparison honestly?
A stakeholder holds you accountable for something a predecessor actually caused. How likely are you to clarify the history rather than absorb blame that isn't yours?
You promised measurable outcomes for an initiative. How likely are you to report the actual measured results, favorable or not?
A mistake you made is minor, but ignoring it sets a bad example. How likely are you to still acknowledge it for the sake of the standard you're setting?
Your team is focused on daily tasks and losing sight of the bigger picture. How likely are you to reconnect their work to the larger vision?
You're setting a direction for your team amid uncertainty. How likely are you to commit to a clear path rather than leave things vague?
A new opportunity doesn't fit your current strategy. How likely are you to evaluate it seriously rather than dismiss it out of hand?
Your team needs a long-term direction, not just short-term wins. How likely are you to invest time in defining that vision?
Market or environmental conditions shift significantly. How likely are you to reassess your strategy rather than stay the course out of habit?
You have a vision that's ambitious and slightly uncomfortable for your team. How likely are you to communicate it with conviction anyway?
A competing priority threatens to derail your team's main strategic goal. How likely are you to protect focus on what matters most?
You could chase every new trend or shiny opportunity without a coherent strategy tying them together. How likely are you to do this?
Your team needs to understand not just what to do, but why. How likely are you to consistently connect tasks to purpose?
You're setting goals for the next year. How likely are you to involve your team in shaping that direction rather than dictate it alone?
Your team's roadmap needs updating based on new market realities. How likely are you to revise it rather than stick to the original plan out of inertia?
A bold strategic bet you're considering carries real risk of failure. How likely are you to pursue it if the potential upside justifies it?
Your team is executing well but on a strategy that's become outdated. How likely are you to question the strategy itself, not just the execution?
You're setting priorities, and everything feels urgent. How likely are you to force real trade-offs rather than try to do it all?
A strategic initiative isn't showing results as fast as hoped. How likely are you to distinguish between 'wrong strategy' and 'needs more time' honestly?
Your team's vision needs to survive a leadership transition. How likely are you to document and communicate it clearly enough to outlast you?
A competitor's move changes the landscape you're operating in. How likely are you to reassess your position rather than assume nothing's changed?
You could keep pursuing a strategy mainly because you already invested heavily in it, even as evidence mounts against it. How likely are you to do this?
Your team needs a simple, memorable way to understand the strategy. How likely are you to invest in making it clear rather than leaving it complex?
A strategic opportunity requires short-term sacrifice for long-term gain. How likely are you to make that case convincingly to your team?
You're setting a vision that requires buy-in from people outside your direct control. How likely are you to invest in building genuine alignment?
Your team's strategy depends on an assumption that might not hold. How likely are you to stress-test that assumption regularly?
A strategic pivot means walking back public commitments. How likely are you to manage that transition transparently rather than quietly?
You have more strategic options than resources to pursue them. How likely are you to make disciplined choices rather than spread thin?
Your team's current strategy conflicts with a value you claim to hold. How likely are you to resolve that inconsistency rather than ignore it?
A strategic decision has both a popular option and a more effective one. How likely are you to choose effectiveness when the difference matters?
You could copy a competitor's strategy wholesale without adapting it to your own context. How likely are you to do this?
Your team needs to understand what you're deliberately choosing not to do. How likely are you to communicate those trade-offs as clearly as the priorities?
A long-term vision requires patience that's being tested by short-term pressure. How likely are you to hold the line when it's the right call?
You're setting strategy in a fast-changing environment. How likely are you to build in regular checkpoints to revisit assumptions?
A strategic initiative you're proud of is being questioned by a new stakeholder. How likely are you to engage with their questions seriously rather than defensively?
Your team's strategy needs input from people closest to the actual work. How likely are you to seek that input rather than decide in isolation?
A strategic goal is technically achievable but would compromise quality or ethics. How likely are you to reject that path even if it's tempting?
You're setting a vision that's genuinely ambitious. How likely are you to pair it with a credible path to get there, not just inspiration?
Your team's strategy hasn't been revisited in a long time. How likely are you to proactively schedule time to reassess it?
You could avoid setting a clear strategy at all, preferring to stay flexible and reactive indefinitely. How likely are you to do this?
A strategic decision requires saying no to a tempting but distracting opportunity. How likely are you to say no in service of focus?
Your team executes a strategy that fails. How likely are you to extract honest lessons rather than rationalize the failure away?
A vision you're setting needs to resonate emotionally, not just logically. How likely are you to invest in making it meaningful, not just correct?
Your strategic plan assumes conditions that have since changed. How likely are you to notice that drift and adjust rather than execute blindly?
A strategic choice benefits the organization long-term but costs you politically short-term. How likely are you to make it anyway?
You're setting direction amid genuine ambiguity about the future. How likely are you to commit to a reasoned bet rather than wait for certainty that won't come?
Your team's strategy needs a name or framing that people can rally around. How likely are you to invest in that communication, not just the substance?
A strategic review reveals your original assumptions were wrong. How likely are you to update the strategy rather than defend the original thinking?
You could set an overly vague strategy on purpose so you're never clearly wrong about anything. How likely are you to do this?
Your team's strategic priorities conflict with another team's. How likely are you to negotiate a resolution rather than just push through unilaterally?
A strategic bet you made is starting to pay off. How likely are you to double down thoughtfully rather than get overconfident?
Your team needs to understand how their daily work ladders up to the strategy. How likely are you to make those connections explicit?
A strategic decision requires input you don't have expertise in. How likely are you to bring in the right expertise rather than guess?
Your team's vision hasn't been revisited since founding. How likely are you to check whether it still fits reality today?
A team member shows potential beyond their current role. How likely are you to invest in developing that potential?
You notice a skills gap on your team. How likely are you to arrange training or mentorship to close it?
A team member wants career growth that might eventually take them beyond your team. How likely are you to support that anyway?
You have limited time, and both your own tasks and your team's development compete for it. How likely are you to still prioritize their growth?
A team member is struggling and needs coaching, not just correction. How likely are you to invest the time to coach them?
You're building a new team from scratch. How likely are you to prioritize complementary skills and diverse perspectives?
A high performer on your team hasn't been challenged in a while. How likely are you to find new challenges to keep them growing?
You could avoid giving a struggling team member honest feedback because the conversation feels uncomfortable. How likely are you to do this?
Your team faces a skills gap that a new hire could fill, but developing an existing member could also work. How likely are you to consider developing from within first?
A team member's confidence is low despite good performance. How likely are you to help them recognize their own growth?
A team member wants to move into a role that doesn't exist yet on your team. How likely are you to consider creating it if it makes sense?
You're building a development plan for someone with unclear career goals. How likely are you to help them explore options rather than assume a path for them?
A team member's growth would benefit from a stretch project outside their comfort zone. How likely are you to offer it despite some risk?
Your team lacks diversity of thought because everyone has a similar background. How likely are you to actively seek different perspectives when hiring or developing?
A team member wants feedback on skills you're not an expert in yourself. How likely are you to connect them with someone who is, rather than guess?
You're mentoring someone who reminds you of your younger self, including your flaws. How likely are you to help them avoid your own past mistakes honestly?
A team member's growth plateaus after early quick wins. How likely are you to help them find the next level of challenge?
You could invest development time and attention only in the team members most likely to make you look good. How likely are you to do this?
You're developing a succession plan and multiple people could be ready in different ways. How likely are you to develop several rather than bet on just one?
A team member needs skills that require them stepping away from daily output for training. How likely are you to protect that time despite short-term cost?
You notice a team member's strengths aren't being used in their current role. How likely are you to look for ways to realign their work?
A team member wants brutally honest feedback about their weaknesses. How likely are you to give it with care rather than either sugarcoat or be needlessly harsh?
Your team's collective skill set has gaps that limit what you can take on. How likely are you to address this proactively through hiring or development?
A team member is ready for more responsibility, but there's no formal next role available. How likely are you to find creative ways to grow them anyway?
You're developing a team member who might eventually surpass your own skill level. How likely are you to support that growth without feeling threatened?
A team member learns best through failure and iteration. How likely are you to give them room to fail safely rather than over-protect them?
You could keep a talented team member under-challenged because their current output is convenient for you. How likely are you to do this?
You're building a mentorship culture on your team. How likely are you to model being mentored yourself, not just mentoring others?
A team member's development goals require resources beyond your budget. How likely are you to advocate upward for that investment?
You notice a team member has untapped leadership potential. How likely are you to create opportunities for them to lead something, even small?
A team member's skill development has stalled due to comfort, not lack of ability. How likely are you to gently push them out of that comfort zone?
You're building a training program from scratch. How likely are you to base it on real skill gaps rather than generic content?
A team member wants to specialize deeply rather than become a generalist. How likely are you to support that path if it fits the team's needs?
Your team's growth has been ignored in favor of pure output for too long. How likely are you to rebalance that going forward?
A team member is a strong individual contributor but resistant to leading others. How likely are you to respect that preference rather than force leadership on them?
You could develop only the people who remind you of yourself, overlooking different but valuable working styles. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding how to split limited training budget across your team. How likely are you to base it on need and potential rather than seniority alone?
A team member's growth requires you to give up some of your own responsibilities to them. How likely are you to do that despite short-term inefficiency?
You notice a team member copying a bad habit from someone senior. How likely are you to address it before it becomes ingrained?
A team member wants honest career advice, even if it means telling them this isn't the right path for them. How likely are you to be that honest?
Your team's skills need updating due to changes in the field. How likely are you to invest in retraining rather than just hire externally?
A team member is developing well but doesn't realize it due to low self-awareness. How likely are you to reflect their progress back to them clearly?
You're asked to rank team members for a single development opportunity. How likely are you to use fair, transparent criteria?
A team member's development would benefit from external training you'd need to approve. How likely are you to say yes if it's genuinely valuable?
You could quietly discourage a team member from taking an external opportunity because you don't want to lose them. How likely are you to do this?
You're building cross-training so the team isn't dependent on single points of failure. How likely are you to prioritize that despite short-term productivity cost?
A team member's confidence needs building more than their skills right now. How likely are you to focus your support accordingly?
You notice uneven development investment across your team. How likely are you to correct that imbalance deliberately?
A team member asks for feedback they can act on immediately, not just praise. How likely are you to give something concrete and useful?
Your team's development plans haven't been revisited in a while. How likely are you to schedule a check-in to update them?
Your team's usual way of working stops being effective. How likely are you to change your leadership approach rather than force the old method?
A crisis requires a very different leadership style than your usual one. How likely are you to adjust to what the moment requires?
Different team members respond to different leadership styles. How likely are you to adapt your approach for each person?
You're leading a team through significant change. How likely are you to adjust your communication frequency to provide more reassurance?
A plan you set for your team needs to change based on new information. How likely are you to pivot rather than stick with it for consistency's sake?
Your team's makeup changes significantly, like through new hires or departures. How likely are you to adjust your leadership approach to the new dynamic?
You're leading in a remote or hybrid setting different from what you're used to. How likely are you to adapt your management style accordingly?
You could apply the exact same leadership style to every team member and situation, regardless of what each actually needs. How likely are you to do this?
Unexpected obstacles derail your team's plan. How likely are you to help the team pivot quickly rather than dwell on the disruption?
A new organizational structure changes your role significantly. How likely are you to adapt your leadership approach to fit it?
Your leadership approach worked well in your last role but isn't landing in this new context. How likely are you to adjust rather than force it?
A team member responds better to autonomy than close guidance. How likely are you to give them more space rather than apply a one-size-fits-all style?
Your organization shifts from rapid growth to stabilization. How likely are you to adjust your leadership style to fit the new phase?
A new generation entering your team has different expectations about work. How likely are you to adapt your approach rather than dismiss the difference?
You're leading through an acquisition or merger with a very different culture. How likely are you to adapt your style to bridge the two?
A crisis calls for more directive leadership than your usual collaborative style. How likely are you to shift gears when the moment calls for it?
Once a crisis passes, your team needs you to return to a more collaborative style. How likely are you to shift back rather than stay in crisis mode?
You could resist changing your leadership style even when clear evidence shows it isn't working anymore. How likely are you to do this?
You're leading a team that's grown much larger than when you started. How likely are you to adjust your involvement level accordingly?
A team member from a different cultural background has different expectations of hierarchy. How likely are you to adapt your approach with them specifically?
Your team's needs shift from execution to innovation. How likely are you to loosen structure to allow for that?
You're leading in an industry undergoing rapid disruption. How likely are you to keep questioning your own assumptions about what works?
A previously successful process stops working as the team scales. How likely are you to redesign it rather than force the old process to fit?
Your usual leadership style isn't resonating with a particular team member. How likely are you to try a different approach specifically for them?
You're asked to lead a team in a function you have little direct experience in. How likely are you to adapt by leaning on their expertise more than usual?
A shift to remote work changes how your team collaborates. How likely are you to adjust your leadership rhythms accordingly?
You could keep leading exactly as you always have, assuming what worked before will always work. How likely are you to do this?
Your team's morale needs a different kind of leadership presence during a hard stretch. How likely are you to show up differently to meet that need?
A merger brings together two teams with different working norms. How likely are you to help forge a new shared approach rather than impose one team's way?
You're stepping into a team with an established culture different from your natural style. How likely are you to adapt initially before trying to change things?
Your organization's priorities shift suddenly due to external events. How likely are you to recalibrate your leadership focus accordingly?
A team member's life circumstances require more flexibility from you as a leader temporarily. How likely are you to provide it?
You're leading a much more diverse team than before. How likely are you to adjust your default assumptions about what motivates people?
Your team moves from a startup phase to a more mature, process-driven phase. How likely are you to embrace the needed structural changes?
A leadership technique that worked with your last team falls flat with this one. How likely are you to diagnose why rather than assume the team is the problem?
You could blame the team for not responding well to your leadership style rather than considering you might need to adapt. How likely are you to do this?
Your team is going through a period of high uncertainty. How likely are you to increase the frequency and clarity of your communication?
You're leading in a context where your formal authority is limited. How likely are you to adapt by leading through influence and relationships instead?
A new tool or technology changes how your team's work happens. How likely are you to adapt your oversight approach to fit the new workflow?
Your team includes both junior and senior members with very different needs. How likely are you to flex your style across that range?
You're leading through a reorganization that changes reporting lines. How likely are you to adapt quickly to the new structure's demands?
A team member's personality clashes with your natural leadership style. How likely are you to find a way to adapt rather than write them off?
Your team's work becomes more global and cross-timezone. How likely are you to adjust your availability and communication patterns?
You're leading through a period where your team needs more emotional support than tactical direction. How likely are you to shift your focus accordingly?
You could stick rigidly to a five-year-old leadership playbook even as the team and context have changed dramatically. How likely are you to do this?
A team member you're leading has more technical expertise than you in a specific area. How likely are you to adapt by deferring to them there?
Your team's size doubles quickly. How likely are you to adjust your span of direct involvement to match?
You're asked to lead in a completely unfamiliar industry. How likely are you to adapt by learning quickly rather than relying solely on past playbooks?
A shift in company strategy changes what's expected of your team. How likely are you to realign your leadership priorities accordingly?
Your team's preferred communication channels have changed over time. How likely are you to adapt to where they actually are rather than insist on the old channel?
You have information that would make your decision easier to sell, but it's not fully accurate. How likely are you to stick to the truth anyway?
Your team needs to trust your word. How likely are you to consistently follow through on what you say?
You're influencing a decision where your own interests and the team's interests slightly diverge. How likely are you to prioritize what's right for the team?
A shortcut would make you look better quickly but isn't the right thing to do. How likely are you to avoid it?
Your team is watching how you handle a tempting ethical gray area. How likely are you to model the standard you expect from them?
You're asked to implement a decision from above that you personally disagree with ethically. How likely are you to raise your concerns through the right channels?
A team member confides something in you in trust. How likely are you to protect that confidence?
You could tell different people different things to keep everyone temporarily happy, even if it's inconsistent. How likely are you to do this?
You're recognized for a success that was really a team effort. How likely are you to make sure credit is shared accurately?
Your team is more likely to follow your example than your instructions. How likely are you to consistently model the behavior you want to see?
A shortcut in reporting numbers would make your team look better to leadership. How likely are you to report the real numbers instead?
You're offered a favor in exchange for preferential treatment toward someone. How likely are you to decline it?
A colleague asks you to cover for something they did that violates policy. How likely are you to decline and address it properly instead?
You're in a position to influence a decision that benefits you but harms the team. How likely are you to choose the team's interest?
Your team is watching how you handle a small ethical lapse from someone you like. How likely are you to hold them to the same standard as anyone else?
You could take an easy win by cutting an ethical corner nobody would notice. How likely are you to avoid it anyway?
A stakeholder pressures you to present overly optimistic projections. How likely are you to present honest ones instead?
You could quietly bend rules for yourself that you strictly enforce for others. How likely are you to do this?
You're asked to keep a confidence that later becomes ethically complicated. How likely are you to navigate that thoughtfully rather than break it carelessly?
A decision you're influencing has a version that's easier to justify and a version that's actually right. How likely are you to choose what's right?
You have leverage over someone due to your position. How likely are you to use it responsibly rather than exploit it?
Your team is more likely to be honest with you if you model that yourself. How likely are you to be transparent even about your own mistakes?
A competitor engages in a practice you consider unethical. How likely are you to resist adopting it just to keep up?
You're asked to influence a decision using information not everyone has access to. How likely are you to disclose that information fairly rather than exploit the asymmetry?
A small act of favoritism would smooth things over in the short term. How likely are you to avoid it for the sake of long-term trust?
You're trusted with sensitive information about layoffs or restructuring before it's public. How likely are you to keep that confidence fully?
You could use your influence to quietly undermine a peer you see as a rival. How likely are you to do this?
Your integrity is tested when following the rules costs you a real opportunity. How likely are you to follow them anyway?
A team member looks to you as a model for how to handle pressure ethically. How likely are you to hold that standard even when it's hard?
You're asked to influence stakeholders using a persuasive but slightly misleading framing. How likely are you to use an honest framing instead?
A decision within your influence could unfairly disadvantage a group that isn't in the room. How likely are you to advocate for them anyway?
You have information that would embarrass a colleague if leaked. How likely are you to protect that information even if you have reason to dislike them?
Your influence over a hiring decision could favor a personal connection over the best candidate. How likely are you to choose the best candidate?
A shortcut in due diligence would speed up a deal you're influencing. How likely are you to insist on doing it properly anyway?
You're asked to publicly defend a decision you privately think was wrong. How likely are you to find an honest way to respond rather than lie?
You could use flattery or manipulation rather than genuine merit to build influence with people above you. How likely are you to do this?
A conflict of interest arises in a decision you're influencing. How likely are you to disclose it transparently rather than hide it?
You're trusted to represent your team's interests in a negotiation. How likely are you to represent them honestly rather than overselling or underselling?
A colleague asks you to vouch for their work without you having actually reviewed it. How likely are you to decline until you've actually checked?
Your influence could be used to quietly settle an old score. How likely are you to keep your decisions free of that motive?
You're asked to weigh in on a sensitive personnel matter. How likely are you to base your input on facts rather than personal feelings?
A team member trusts you with a mistake they haven't told anyone else about. How likely are you to help them address it rather than use it against them later?
Your influence grows as your role grows. How likely are you to remain as accountable as when you had less power?
A shortcut to building trust quickly would involve overpromising. How likely are you to build trust slowly and honestly instead?
You could exaggerate your own achievements when your influence and reputation are on the line. How likely are you to do this?
You're asked to use your influence to help someone who doesn't actually deserve it in a given situation. How likely are you to decline gracefully?
A team member did something admirable that reflects well on your leadership. How likely are you to make sure they get individual recognition, not just you?
You have the influence to quietly bury a mistake that would otherwise come to light. How likely are you to surface it instead?
Your reputation for integrity is being tested by a situation with real stakes. How likely are you to act consistently with that reputation rather than protect it superficially?
A younger leader is watching how you handle power and influence. How likely are you to model the kind of leadership you'd want them to become?
